    base:desc "Temperature sensor reporting joint temperature for thermal effects on friction."
    DTDFVocab:producedData TemperatureDataTransmitted
]

]

// C10: Data (processed)

// C11: Enablers
instance ProcessingEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Processing component that consumes input traces, applies physics‑based or neural‑network models, and emits output traces for the digital shadow."
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeFrictionMonitoring,
                     PredictiveFrictionModeling,
                     FrictionCompensationControl,
                     PredictiveMaintenanceService,
                     FaultDetectionService,
                     SimulationOptimizationService,
                     ProductQualityPredictionService,
                     DataVisualizationService
]

instance AASExecutorEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "AAS Executor pushes measured, simulated, and reference values into the ThingsBoard database."
]

instance ThingsBoardEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "ThingsBoard instance stores all traces and model results for historical analysis and real‑time updates."
]

instance MQTTBrokerEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MQTT broker (e.g., Mosquitto) forwards JSON messages from sensors to the AAS Executor and ThingsBoard."
]

]

]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The DT operates in (near) real‑time: sensor data from the robot are streamed continuously to the digital shadow via MQTT/ThingsBoard; the processing pipeline runs the friction simulation models and updates the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) with a latency that must respect the control‑system dead time. Synchronisation follows the live data stream frequency (i.e., the sensor sampling rate) and the temporal granularity is at the level of individual data traces, enabling dynamic adaptation of both white‑box (physics‑based) and black‑box (data‑driven) friction models."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The system follows a pipeline orchestration: physical robot → IoT sensors → MQTT broker → ThingsBoard storage → Asset Administration Shell executor → processing components (simulation models in Simulink, data‑calculation models) → updated digital shadow → visualization dashboard. Multi‑domain coupling is achieved by integrating SysML structural models with Simulink behavioural models via the FMI standard. Resource management and automation are provided by the AAS executor, which autonomously triggers simulations, collects results, and forwards them to the shadow and dashboard."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "According to Document 1, the development of a digital twin follows the typical Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) lifecycle that is split into several abstraction levels: (1) requirements engineering, (2) functional architecture, (3) logical architecture, and (4) product/technical layer. At each level the system is represented by appropriate artifacts – SysML requirement and block diagrams, architecture description languages (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc) for component‑connector structures, state‑machine or automaton models for behavior, and finally executable simulation models (e.g., Simulink) or source code (C++, Java). Transition between stages is managed by a concretization step that maps reference‑model concepts to concrete class‑diagram elements, establishing a conformance relation (Document 2). Governance of the lifecycle is implied by the need to maintain traceability of data traces back to the originating processing component and models (Document 2)."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "Model fidelity is ensured by combining validated white‑box Simulink simulations with continuously trained black‑box neural‑network models, and by enforcing traceability of transformations from SysML (or other MBSE artefacts) to simulation models. Verification activities include correctness checks of model transformations, as highlighted for SysML‑to‑simulation pipelines, and uncertainty quantification arising from sensor noise and model approximation. Real‑time constraints such as control‑system dead time are considered to guarantee that model updates (parameter identification and retraining) occur within the permissible latency for high‑precision robot control. Error detection relies on monitoring anomalies in friction behaviour, enabling predictive maintenance and early fault detection. Quality assurance is achieved through continuous validation of model outputs against measured data, automatic updating of model parameters via the Processing component, and maintaining provenance via the origin‑traceability links."
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The communication backbone consists of an MQTT publish‑subscribe network. Sensors send data to a preprocessing component, which connects to an MQTT broker (Mosquitto) that converts the data to JSON and distributes them via hierarchical topics. The AAS Executor subscribes to the relevant topics and retrieves the data. Topics can be assigned read/write permissions to control access. The system also uses the ThingsBoard IoT platform for storage and visualization, which connects to the same MQTT broker. No explicit bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocol (e.g., TLS) details are provided."
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "The digital shadow is built on top of an Asset Administration Shell that serves as the single source of truth. A relational database is configured according to the DS data model and stores the AAS submodels, SysML identification points, and FMU simulation artefacts. The AAS Executor component runs locally, interprets the AAS and executes the simulation models (FMUs exported from Simulink). Processed data are sent to a ThingsBoard dashboard for visualization. The deployment is on‑premise (database, MQTT broker, AAS Executor) with no cloud or edge specifics mentioned; scalability and availability mechanisms are not described."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "Integration with external systems is realized through industry‑4.0 standards: data exchange uses MQTT; asset description and service interfaces use the Asset Administration Shell (AAS); persistent storage and visualisation employ ThingsBoard. Model interoperability is ensured by FMI linking SysML and Simulink models. The digital shadow can be extended by other applications via the AAS and ThingsBoard APIs, enabling interoperability with enterprise MES, production planning, or other Digital Twins."
]
